examples.title: Pseudocode: Layered OS Architecture
examples.description: Demonstrates the layered approach where each layer only communicates with adjacent layers
examples.code:
ALGORITHM Layered_OS_Call
// Demonstrates how a user request traverses OS layers
INPUT: user_request (system call number, parameters)
OUTPUT: result from the requested service

BEGIN
 // Layer N: User Application makes a system call
 request <- CREATE_SYSTEM_CALL(user_request)
 
 // Layer N-1: System Call Interface
 request <- VALIDATE_AND_FORMAT(request)
 
 // Layer N-2: Service Layer (e.g., File System)
 IF request.type = "FILE_OPEN" THEN
 result <- FILE_SYSTEM_SERVICE(request)
 ELSE IF request.type = "MEMORY_ALLOC" THEN
 result <- MEMORY_SERVICE(request)
 END IF
 
 // Layer 2: Memory Management
 IF result.requires_memory THEN
 result <- MMU_MANAGE(result)
 END IF
 
 // Layer 1: CPU Scheduling
 result <- SCHEDULER_PROCESS(result)
 
 // Layer 0: Hardware Interface
 result <- HARDWARE_EXECUTE(result)
 
 RETURN result
END

// Each layer can only call the layer directly below it
ALGORITHM LAYER_N_CALL(layer_number, data)
BEGIN
 IF layer_number > 0 THEN
 // Only communicate with adjacent layer
 LAYER_N_CALL(layer_number - 1, data)
 ELSE
 // Layer 0 interacts with hardware
 HARDWARE_ACCESS(data)
 END IF
END
examples.explanation: This pseudocode shows how a system call traverses through OS layers from top to bottom. Each layer (N to 0) only uses services from the layer directly below it, demonstrating the key principle of the layered approach. This isolation makes debugging easier and provides abstraction.

examples.title: Python: Microkernel Message Passing
examples.description: Demonstrates the microkernel architecture with IPC between user-space services
examples.code:
"""
Microkernel Architecture Simulation
Demonstrates message passing between user-level services
"""

import time
from enum import Enum
from dataclasses import dataclass
from typing import Optional
import queue
import threading

# Message types for Inter-Process Communication
class MessageType(Enum):
 FILE_READ = "FILE_READ"
 FILE_WRITE = "FILE_WRITE"
 MEMORY_ALLOC = "MEMORY_ALLOC"
 PROCESS_CREATE = "PROCESS_CREATE"
 IPC_MESSAGE = "IPC_MESSAGE"

@dataclass
class Message:
 """Message structure for IPC between microkernel services"""
 msg_type: MessageType
 sender: str
 receiver: str
 payload: dict
 timestamp: float

class Microkernel:
 """Core microkernel - only runs essential services"""
 
 def __init__(self):
 self.message_queue = queue.Queue()
 self.running_services = {}
 self.memory_pages = {} # Basic memory management
 
 def send_message(self, msg: Message):
 """Kernel-level message routing"""
 print(f"[KERNEL] Routing message from {msg.sender} to {msg.receiver}")
 self.message_queue.put(msg)
 
 def route_message(self):
 """Route message to appropriate user-space service"""
 if not self.message_queue.empty():
 msg = self.message_queue.get()
 if msg.receiver in self.running_services:
 self.running_services[msg.receiver].process_message(msg)
 
 def register_service(self, service_name: str, service):
 """Register a user-space service"""
 self.running_services[service_name] = service
 print(f"[KERNEL] Registered service: {service_name}")

class UserSpaceService:
 """Base class for user-level services (file system, networking, etc.)"""
 
 def __init__(self, name: str, kernel: Microkernel):
 self.name = name
 self.kernel = kernel
 
 def send(self, receiver: str, msg_type: MessageType, payload: dict):
 """Send message to another service via kernel"""
 msg = Message(
 msg_type=msg_type,
 sender=self.name,
 receiver=receiver,
 payload=payload,
 timestamp=time.time()
 )
 self.kernel.send_message(msg)
 
 def process_message(self, msg: Message):
 """Process incoming message - to be overridden"""
 print(f"[{self.name}] Received: {msg.msg_type}")

class FileSystemService(UserSpaceService):
 """File System running in user space"""
 
 def __init__(self, kernel: Microkernel):
 super().__init__("FileSystem", kernel)
 self.files = {}
 
 def process_message(self, msg: Message):
 if msg.msg_type == MessageType.FILE_READ:
 filename = msg.payload.get("filename")
 content = self.files.get(filename, "File not found")
 print(f"[{self.name}] Reading file: {filename}")
 # Send response back
 self.send(msg.sender, MessageType.IPC_MESSAGE, {"content": content})
 elif msg.msg_type == MessageType.FILE_WRITE:
 filename = msg.payload.get("filename")
 content = msg.payload.get("content")
 self.files[filename] = content
 print(f"[{self.name}] Writing file: {filename}")

class NetworkService(UserSpaceService):
 """Network service running in user space"""
 
 def __init__(self, kernel: Microkernel):
 super().__init__("Network", kernel)
 
 def process_message(self, msg: Message):
 print(f"[{self.name}] Processing network request: {msg.payload}")

# Demo execution
if __name__ == "__main__":
 # Create microkernel (only essential kernel code)
 kernel = Microkernel()
 
 # Start user-space services (in real system, these would be separate processes)
 file_service = FileSystemService(kernel)
 network_service = NetworkService(kernel)
 
 # Register services with microkernel
 kernel.register_service("FileSystem", file_service)
 kernel.register_service("Network", network_service)
 
 print("\n--- Microkernel Demo: File operations via message passing ---\n")
 
 # Simulate application requesting file operation
 app_msg = Message(
 msg_type=MessageType.FILE_WRITE,
 sender="Application",
 receiver="FileSystem",
 payload={"filename": "test.txt", "content": "Hello OS Structure!"},
 timestamp=time.time()
 )
 kernel.send_message(app_msg)
 kernel.route_message()
 
 # Read the file
 read_msg = Message(
 msg_type=MessageType.FILE_READ,
 sender="Application",
 receiver="FileSystem",
 payload={"filename": "test.txt"},
 timestamp=time.time()
 )
 kernel.send_message(read_msg)
 kernel.route_message()
 
 print("\n--- If FileSystem crashes, kernel and other services continue ---")
 print("[DEMO] FileSystem service would be killed but Network still works")
 kernel.running_services.pop("FileSystem")
 kernel.send_message(Message(MessageType.IPC_MESSAGE, "Test", "Network", {}, 0))
 kernel.route_message()
examples.explanation: This Python code demonstrates the microkernel architecture where essential services (IPC, basic memory, scheduling) run in kernel space, while file system, networking, and other services run as user-level processes. Services communicate through message passing via the microkernel. This shows how a crashed user-service doesn't bring down the entire OS.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for message routing, where n is number of services
examples.spaceComplexity: O(s) for service registry, s = number of services

examples.title: C: Modular OS Kernel Structure
examples.description: Demonstrates a modular kernel design with separate modules for memory, process, and file management
examples.explanation: This C code demonstrates a modular OS kernel design where different subsystems (process management, memory management, file system) are implemented as separate modules. Each module has initialization and cleanup functions. This mirrors how Linux loadable kernel modules (LKMs) work, allowing functionality to be added or removed without rebooting.
examples.timeComplexity: O(n) for module initialization, O(p) for process operations
examples.spaceComplexity: O(m + p + f) where m=memory pages, p=processes, f=files
